answer:
**Vṛhaspatitattva's metaphysical distinction between *cetana* (conscious) and *acetana* (unconscious) is foundational and explicit: the supreme *tattva* (principle of reality) is twofold, *cetana* and *acetana*, from which all other *tattvas* arise.**[^1] *Cetana* is defined as "that which has the nature of knowledge, which knows and is never subject to forgetting, which is eternally aware at all times, and which is unobscured"; *acetana* "is that which is without knowledge, like a stone."[^2] This binary pervades the cosmology: pure *cetana* (*Paramaśivatattva*, etc.) is eternal and sovereign, but individual souls (*ātmā*, a lower *cetana*) become "dazed consciousness" when stained by *mala* (impurity) in *māyātattva*, losing all-knowing/all-doing powers and falling into forgetfulness via *pradhānatattva* (unconscious mind-stuff).[^3] Souls cycle through *saṃsāra* (rebirth), even devolving into *sthāvara* (immobile, stone-like) states resembling *acetana*. Liberation (*mokṣa*) requires yoga to recover unobscured awareness.

**Charles Comte presumes humans as inherently rational agents bound by natural laws to rights/duties, with liberty as their precondition.**[^4] Man "by his nature... is bound to nothing, neither toward himself, nor toward others," yet this yields reciprocal duties (e.g., respecting others' liberty) inherent to human nature, making slavery ("treating [man] as a property") a violation.[^5] Liberty is "the state of a person who encounters... no obstacle... to the regular development of his being, or to the innocent exercise of his faculties," presupposing self-aware persons with faculties to develop.[^6]

**Dunoyer similarly inherits an already-constituted rational subject, defining liberty as acquired *power* over faculties (intelligence, will, organs) via culture, distinguishing it from natural impediments (ignorance, vice).**[^7] Man is "a being subject to needs, and provided with faculties to satisfy them," free insofar as developed/regulated against self-harm or harming others; savages are *less* free than civilized due to undeveloped faculties.[^8]

**Vṛhaspatitattva would refuse Comte's category of the human as an inherently *free person* with presumptively rational, self-aware nature.** Comte takes "person" (vs. "property/thing") for granted as axiomatic to liberty/rights, grounded in unexamined human "nature" opposing slavery.[^9] Vṛhaspatitattva denies this: souls (*ātmā*) are not stably "persons" but dazed *cetana* trapped in *acetana* (*māyā*, *pradhāna*), forgetting their nature and devolving into animal/plant/stone states.[^10] Rationality/self-awareness is not innate but obscured by *mala*/*vāsanā*; recovery demands *ṣaḍaṅgayoga* (six-limbed yoga), not mere anti-slavery politics.[^11] Comte's "person" blurs *cetana*/*acetana*, presuming purity without metaphysical discernment.

[^1]: Vṛhaspatitattva, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-04d69c](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-04d69c).
[^2]: Vṛhaspatitattva, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-c31df3](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-c31df3).
[^3]: Vṛhaspatitattva, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-643885](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-643885).
[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Ch. 2, paragraph p-3114c0](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-3114c0).
[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Ch. 2, full chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/).
[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Ch. 3, paragraph p-9b6829](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-9b6829).
[^7]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Ch. 1, paragraph p-fc8999](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).
[^8]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Ch. 1, full chapter](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/).
[^9]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Ch. 3, paragraph p-86c544](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544).
[^10]: Vṛhaspatitattva, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-7f742a](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-7f742a).
[^11]: Vṛhaspatitattva, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-724e96](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-724e96).
